ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for sushi as a food item in standard clinical terminology.

main_ingredients: raw fish (salmon, tuna, or other sashimi-grade fish)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw fish sushi consists of approximately 6 nigiri or maki pieces, weighing around 150g, commonly consumed as a light meal or starter.

nutrient_source.title: Sushi (raw) — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sushi (raw), CR ID F05100135. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; sushi rice / vinegared white rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value for composite raw fish sushi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from vinegared sushi rice, which is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002; Sugiyama et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2003). Vinegar in sushi rice lowers the GI of white rice from approximately 72 to approximately 54–56. The raw fish topping contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. GL per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (23.32g × 1.5 = 34.98g) / 100 = 19.2. A GI of 55 is classified as 'low' according to the University of Sydney GI Database (low GI < 55, medium GI 56-69, high GI > 70).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 338mg/100g with low potassium offset (~110mg/100g) and low dietary fibre (0.65g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit sushi portions and strictly avoid or minimise soy sauce dipping, as a single tablespoon of regular soy sauce adds approximately 900mg sodium, which can substantially exceed the recommended daily sodium ceiling of 2000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Vinegared sushi rice has a low GI (~55) with 23.3g carbohydrate per 100g and very low dietary fibre (0.65g/100g), producing a glycaemic load of approximately 19 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 4–6 pieces per sitting, pair sushi with edamame or miso soup to slow glucose absorption, and consider sashimi (fish without rice) as a lower-carbohydrate alternative for better postprandial glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Raw fish toppings (salmon, tuna) provide omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) with low saturated fat (~0.4g/100g) and no trans fat, supporting a cardioprotective profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw fish sushi is generally suitable for cardiovascular health due to its omega-3 content and low saturated fat; however, patients should minimise soy sauce use to manage sodium intake and avoid exceeding 2–3 servings per week to limit cumulative sodium exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) from raw fish toppings reduce joint inflammation; low saturated fat and no trans fat support anti-inflammatory classification
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw fish sushi is beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis due to its omega-3 content; however, immunocompromised patients on biologics or methotrexate should consult their treating physician before consuming raw fish due to infection risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sushi (raw fish) is a traditional Japanese dish consisting of vinegared short-grain rice topped with slices of raw sashimi-grade fish such as salmon, tuna, yellowtail, or snapper. The rice is seasoned with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt, giving it a mildly tangy and slightly sweet flavour. The raw fish topping provides a clean, delicate, and umami-rich taste with a smooth, tender texture that contrasts with the slightly sticky rice. Sushi is typically served with pickled ginger (gari), wasabi, and soy sauce for dipping. Nutritionally, raw fish sushi provides 139 kcal per 100g, with 7.1g protein from the fish, 23.3g carbohydrate primarily from the rice, and only 2.0g fat, making it a relatively low-calorie, moderate-protein meal. The vinegar in sushi rice lowers its glycaemic index to approximately 55 (low GI), compared to plain white rice at GI ~72. Sodium is notable at 338mg/100g before any soy sauce addition. The raw fish component is rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are clinically relevant for reducing cardiovascular risk and joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis. Sushi is widely consumed in Japan and globally, and is popular in Singapore as a mainstream dining option. It is suitable for low-calorie dietary patterns but requires caution for individuals managing hypertension due to sodium content, and moderate caution for type 2 diabetes due to the low glycaemic index but significant carbohydrate load per serving.
common_user_queries: Can I eat sushi with high blood pressure?
What is the GI of sushi rice?
Is sushi good for heart health?
How much protein is in sushi?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05100135) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from vinegared sushi rice using University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Foster-Powell et al.; Sugiyama et al.), which is the closest defensible approved-source analogue. Saturated fat estimated at 0.4g/100g consistent with the health_suitability input data. Potassium estimated at ~110mg/100g from USDA FoodData Central analogous sushi entry as HPB does not report potassium for this item.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite sushi (raw) entry are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05100135) as of 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central does not carry an exact match for this composite dish. Saturated fat value of 0.4g/100g is carried forward from the input health_suitability data and is consistent with the low-fat profile of raw fish. Potassium value of 110mg/100g is an estimate from USDA FoodData Central analogous sushi entry (NDB No. 15228) and is used only for clinical context in the hypertension health note. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on vinegared sushi rice per University of Sydney GI Database and Sugiyama et al. (Am J Clin Nutr 2003); no direct composite GI value exists for raw fish sushi in approved sources. Halal status is marked false as standard sushi preparations may include mirin (rice wine) in the rice seasoning; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant or product. The input record listed 'diabetes' as the condition key; this has been updated to 'type2_diabetes' per Veritas naming rules. The gi_category has been corrected from 'medium' to 'low' as per University of Sydney GI Database classification for a GI of 55.
